elements.sent-2411-001: In 1971, Davies proved that finitely many parallel line segments can be simultaneously fully rotated in an arbitrarily small area.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this paper we show that an even stronger statement holds: The unit square can be fully rotated in such a way that each initially vertical line segment sweeps a set of small area.
elements.sent-2411-003: A set in $\mathbb{R ^n$ is said to have the strong Kakeya property if for any two of its positions, the set can be continuously moved between these two positions in an arbitrarily small area.}
elements.sent-2411-004: We use the above result to show that a wide family of sets in $\mathbb{R ^3$, for instance the curved surface of a cylinder, have the strong Kakeya property.}
elements.sent-2411-005: In 1917, Kakeya posed a question now known as the Kakeya needle problem: Among sets, in which a line segment of unit length can be fully rotated, which one has minimal area? Besicovitch \cite{besi showed that there is no such minimum, the area of such a set can be arbitrarily small.}
elements.sent-2411-006: In 1971, Davies \cite{Da demonstrated that more is true: finitely many parallel line segments can be simultaneously rotated, in an arbitrarily small area.}
elements.sent-2411-007: Our main result is even stronger:.
elements.sent-2411-008: For every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists a continuous motion of the unit square during which every initially vertical line segment sweeps at most $\varepsilon$ area, while the square does a full rotation.
elements.sent-2411-009: A set in $\mathbb{R ^n$ is said to have the Kakeya property if it has two positions, between which the set can be continuously moved in an arbitrarily small area \cite{kapo .}}
elements.sent-2411-010: It is said to have the strong Kakeya property if this applies to any two of its positions.
elements.sent-2411-011: It is easy to show that the result of Davies \cite{Da implies that a finite union of parallel line segments possess the strong Kakeya property.}
elements.sent-2411-012: In the same paper he showed that the segments must be parallel, two non-parallel segments do not have the strong Kakeya property.
elements.sent-2411-013: Much effort has been put into characterizing sets in $\mathbb{R ^2$ with the Kakeya property and the strong Kakeya property.. M.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Laczkovich \cite{kapo showed that closed connected set with the Kakeya property must be a subset of a line or circle.. K.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Laczkovich \cite{hera2016 proved that short enough circular arcs do in fact possess the strong Kakeya property.}
elements.sent-2411-018: Currently the strongest version of this statement is due to A.. Chang and M.
elements.sent-2411-020: They generalized the result of Davies in another direction by showing that a countable union of parallel line segments which is bounded and has finite total length can be rotated in an arbitrarily small area.
elements.sent-2411-021: It is still unknown whether all non-complete circular arcs possess the strong Kakeya property.
elements.sent-2411-022: Let $V$ be the set of planes, which have a $45^\circ$ angle with the $\{y=0\}$ plane.
elements.sent-2411-023: In Section~\ref{s:2 we will prove the following lemma.}
elements.sent-2411-024: There exists a closed set $A$ in $\mathbb{R ^3$, which is the union of planes in $V$ at a bounded distance from the origin, contains a translate of every plane in $V$, and intersects every vertical line in a set of measure zero.}
elements.sent-2411-025: The proof relies on a duality argument and utilizes the construction of Talagrand \cite{talagrand , for an English description see Appendix A.. in \cite{HANSEN .}}
elements.sent-2411-026: Lemma~\ref{t:folemma is closely related to the question of rotating a square.}
elements.sent-2411-027: The connection can be seen if we think of the plane as the $\{y=0\}$ plane in $\mathbb{R ^3$ and raise every vertical segment of the unit square by its distance from the origin.}
elements.sent-2411-028: During the motion of the unit square, the motion of each initially vertical segment corresponds to the motion of the raised rectangle at a given height.
elements.sent-2411-029: Thus Lemma~\ref{t:folemma can be thought of as giving a discontinuous motion of the unit square, during which it achieves every direction.}
elements.sent-2411-030: In Section~\ref{s:rot we turn this motion into a continuous one and prove Theorem~\ref{t:mainresult .}}
elements.sent-2411-031: Section~\ref{s:sk will discuss how Theorem~\ref{t:mainresult can be applied to show that certain sets in $\mathbb{R ^3$ have the strong Kakeya property.}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: To demonstrate the connecting idea, consider the curved surface of a cylinder with an axis parallel to the $z$-axis.
elements.sent-2411-033: If we look at this set from the direction of the $y$-axis then we see a subset of a square.
elements.sent-2411-034: We can apply a motion given by Theorem~\ref{t:mainresult that rotates this square by $90^\circ$.}
elements.sent-2411-035: This will define a motion on the surface as well.
elements.sent-2411-036: During the motion of the surface the area swept in the $\{ y=h \}$ plane will be at most the sum of the areas swept by the segments in this plane, which is less than $\varepsilon$ for each segment.
elements.sent-2411-037: Therefore, this motion moved the surface of the cylinder with an axis parallel to the $z$-axis to one with an axis parallel to the $x$-axis in an arbitrarily small volume.
elements.sent-2411-038: This idea applies to a much wider family of sets, not just the curved surface of a cylinder.
elements.sent-2411-039: We say that a compact set $K\subset \mathbb{R ^3$ is cylinderlike if there exists $n\in \mathbb{N}$ such that for almost all $t$ the set $\{x=t\}\cap K$ can be covered by $n$ vertical lines.}
elements.sent-2411-040: We say that a compact set $K$ is cylinderlike from direction $d$ if there is an orthogonal coordinate system, in which the $x$-axis is parallel to $d$, and $K$ is cylinderlike.
elements.sent-2411-041: If $K$ is cylinderlike from two non-parallel directions $d_1, d_2$, then $K$ has the strong Kakeya property.
elements.sent-2411-042: It follows that the union of a finite number of parallel curved cylinder surfaces possesses the strong Kakeya property.
elements.sent-2411-043: The above theorem also implies that a compact subset of any two planes has the strong Kakeya property.
elements.sent-2411-044: More general examples of such sets are given at the end of Section~\ref{s:sk .}

elements.section-2411-003: Rotating a square, the proof of Theorem~\ref{t:mainresult}
elements.sent-2411-119: Call a rectangle in $\mathbb{R ^3$ $interesting$ if its sides are of length $1$ and $\sqrt{2}$, one of the shorter sides lies in the plane $\{y=0\}$, it has a $45^\circ$ angle with the $\{ y=0\}$ plane and is in the $y \ge 0$ half-space.}
elements.sent-2411-120: By the neighbourhood of an interesting rectangle we will mean a set that contains the rectangle and is relatively open in $\mathbb{R}\times [0,1]\times \mathbb{R}$.
elements.sent-2411-121: For every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists a set $U$ such that for every possible direction $U$ contains a neighbourhood of an interesting rectangle in that direction, while it intersects the $\{y=h\}$ plane in a set of measure less than $\varepsilon$ for all $h$.
elements.sent-2411-122: Let $A$ be the set provided by Lemma \ref{t:folemma .}
elements.sent-2411-123: Fix an N large enough that \begin{equation}A \cap ((-N,N) \times [0,1] \times (-N,N))\end{equation} contains an interesting rectangle in every direction.
elements.sent-2411-124: For any $\delta > 0$ let $A_\delta$ be $A + \{(-\delta,\delta) \times \{0\} \times (-\delta,\delta) \}$, where + denotes the Minkowski sum and define $A_0$ to be $A$.
elements.sent-2411-125: The set $A_\delta$ is open for all $\delta>0$, since $A$ is a union of planes and we replace each plane with an open set.. Define \begin{equation}B_\delta = A_\delta \cap ([-N,N] \times [0,1] \times [-N,N]).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-126: The set $B_\delta$ is a relatively open in $[-N,N] \times [0,1] \times [-N,N]$ for all $\delta>0$, therefore $B_\delta$ contains a neighbourhood of an interesting rectangle in every possible direction.
elements.sent-2411-128: Using the fact that $B_0$ is compact, it is easy to verify that the function $f_\delta(h)$ is continuous in $\delta$.
elements.sent-2411-129: Since $f_0(h)=0, f_\delta(h)$ converges to 0 as $\delta$ tends to 0.
elements.sent-2411-130: We claim that \begin{equation}\label{e:suc} f_{\delta+t}(h_1) \ge f_{\delta}(h_2)\end{equation} whenever $|h_1 - h_2| < t$.
elements.sent-2411-131: Indeed for every point $p$ in $B_\delta \cap \{ y= h_2 \}$ the set $A$ contains a plane that intersects $\{ y= h_2 \}$ in a line, which is less distance away from $p$ than $\delta$.
elements.sent-2411-132: This plane has a $45^\circ$ angle with the $\{ y= h_2 \}$ plane and intersects $\{ y= h_1 \}$ as well.
elements.sent-2411-134: Hence $f_\frac{1 {n}$ is a sequence of upper semicontinuous functions on $[0,1]$, which is pointwise monotonically decreasing, and pointwise converges to 0.}
elements.sent-2411-135: It is easy to prove that such a sequence must uniformly converge to 0.
elements.sent-2411-136: Therefore there exists a $\delta_0 > 0$ such that $U=B_{\delta_0}$ has all the required properties.
elements.sent-2411-138: Call a continuous motion M of $R$ interesting if at every moment it keeps $R$ interesting.
elements.sent-2411-139: For all $\varepsilon>0$ there exists an interesting motion of $R$, during which $R$ does a full a rotation, but $R \cap \{ y=t\}$ sweeps an area less than $\varepsilon$ for all $t$.
elements.sent-2411-140: We will use the idea of Pál joins to move between translated copies.
elements.sent-2411-141: We claim that for any interesting rectangle $R'$ parallel to $R$ there exists an interesting motion of $R$ during which $R\cap\{y=t\}$ sweeps less than $\varepsilon$ area for all $t$ and $R$ is translated to $R'$.
elements.sent-2411-142: There is a direction in which we can translate $R$ in such a way, that it sweeps 0 area at every height, call this its free direction.
elements.sent-2411-143: We will move $R$ in an N-like shape.
elements.sent-2411-144: We translate it in its free direction, there we rotate it by a small angle, then we translate it in its free direction, and rotate it in the opposite direction, by the same angle.
elements.sent-2411-145: If we translate it far enough, then the angle of rotation can be arbitrarily small, and so the area swept at each height will be small.
elements.sent-2411-146: Let $U$ be the set given by Claim~\ref{c:lu .}
elements.sent-2411-147: For any possible direction $d$, $U$ contains the neighbourhood of an interesting rectangle $R_d$, which is in the direction of $d$.
elements.sent-2411-148: For each $R_d$, there is an interesting motion, that rotates the rectangle a small amount within $U$.
elements.sent-2411-149: Since $S^1$ is compact, we can choose finitely many directions, whose neighbourhoods cover all directions.
elements.sent-2411-150: We have seen that we can move between parallel copies of $R$ while sweeping an arbitrarily small area at all heights, therefore these will only add an arbitrarily small area to the arbitrarily small area of $U$ at each height.
elements.sent-2411-152: If at every moment we project $R$ onto the $\{ y=0\}$ plane, then we get $M'$, a motion of the unit square.
elements.sent-2411-153: Observe that during $M'$ the segment at $t$ distance from $T$ sweeps the same area, as $R \cap \{ y=t\}$ does during $M$.
elements.section-2411-004: Sets with the strong Kakeya property in $\mathbb{R}
elements.sent-2411-154: If $\phi$ is a rotation around the $x$-axis, $K$ is cylinderlike, then $K$ can be moved to $\phi(K)$ in an arbitrarily small volume.
elements.sent-2411-155: We will look at $K$ from the direction of the $x$-axis, and give a motion that keeps the $x$-coordinate of every point constant.
elements.sent-2411-156: The set $K$ is bounded, so its projection onto the $\{x=0\}$ plane can be covered by a square.
elements.sent-2411-158: This naturally induces a motion on $K$.
elements.sent-2411-159: For almost all planes perpendicular to the $x$-axis the swept area will be less than $n\varepsilon$, since if a plane contains n vertical segments, then the motion of each segment corresponds to the motion of a vertical segment of the square and these all sweep an area less than $\varepsilon$.

elements.sent-2411-161: It is easy to check that the proof of the above lemma also works for general cylinderlike sets defined as follows: For a compact set $K\subset \mathbb{R ^3$ let $n(t)$ be the minimum (possibly infinite) number of vertical lines required to cover $K \cap \{ x= t\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-162: Say that $K$ is general cylinderlike if $\int_\mathbb{R n(t) dt < \infty$.}
elements.sent-2411-163: Recall that we say that a compact set $K$ is cylinderlike from direction $d$ if there is an orthogonal coordinate system, in which the $x$-axis is parallel to $d$, and $K$ is cylinderlike.
elements.sent-2411-164: By Lemma $\ref{t:cylinderlike}$: If $K$ is cylinderlike from direction $d$, then $K$ can be rotated around $d$ in an arbitrarily small volume.

elements.sent-2411-168: From this point forward every distance is the arc distance on $S^2$.
elements.sent-2411-169: By Lemma~\ref{t:cylinderlike we can rotate $K$ around the vectors $d_1, d_2$ in an arbitrarily small volume.}
elements.sent-2411-170: Therefore, it is enough to solve the following problem: Take $S^2$ and stick two needles ($n_1$ and $ n_2$) into it, at directions $d_1$ and $d_2$ respectively.
elements.sent-2411-171: The following step is allowed: take a needle and rotate the sphere around it.
elements.sent-2411-173: We need to prove that we can get the needles to any pair of fixed points $p_1$ and $p_2$, which are $t$ distance apart, in a finite number of steps.
elements.sent-2411-174: First case: Suppose $n_1$ and $p_1$ are further than $t$ apart.
elements.sent-2411-175: Then we rotate around $n_1$ in a way such that $n_2$ moves onto a short arc between $n_1 $ and $ p_1$.
elements.sent-2411-177: It is clear that after a finite number of steps $n_1$ and $ p_1$ will be at most $t$ distance apart.
elements.sent-2411-178: Second case: Suppose $n_1, p_1$ are at most $t$ apart.
elements.sent-2411-179: We rotate around $n_1$ in a way such that $n_2$ moves onto the circle centred around $p_1$ of radius $t$.
elements.sent-2411-180: We can rotate around $n_2$ in such a way that $n_1$ moves into $p_1$.
elements.sent-2411-181: Now we can rotate $n_2$ into $p_2$ by rotating around $n_1$.
elements.sent-2411-182: The set $A\times[0,1]$ is cylinderlike from directions $d_1$ and $d_2$, therefore by Theorem~\ref{t:fogantyu it has the strong Kakeya property.}
elements.sent-2411-183: The graph of a Lipchitz function intersects every steep enough line in one point, therefore we can use Corollary~\ref{c:axs .}
elements.sent-2411-184: Under such conditions $A$ intersects every horizontal and vertical line in at most $n$ points, where $n$ is the number of monotonic functions required to cover $A$.
elements.sent-2411-186: Moreover, the finite union of parallel curved cylinder surfaces possesses the strong Kakeya property.
